coredata.dc:title: Revealing the in-situ growth mechanism of carbon dots confined in ZIF-8 as multicolor fluorescent material with high photothermal stability
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhi
Yan, Lingpeng
Song, Zhengshu
Zhang, Jiyun
Yang, Yongzhen
Liu, Xuguang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) based fluorescent materials offer tremendous potential for application in high-definition displays and advanced illumination systems. However, achieving solid-state CDs-based phosphors with robust photothermal stability remains a critical challenge. In this study, multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 phosphors were synthesized by precisely controlling the carbonation degree of CDs under the confinement effect of ZIF-8. Serving as both template and a protective substrate, ZIF-8 enables the in-situ carbonation of the carbon source into CDs embedded within its pores through a solvothermal process. Structural and compositional analyses following HCl etching of the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 phosphors showed that the in-situ growth of CDs in ZIF-8 pores. The multicolor emission of the CDs is attributed to the quantum size effect. The rigid structure of the ZIF-8 backbone imparts exceptional photothermal stability to the CDs@ZIF-8 phosphors. As a result, the materials exhibit stable fluorescence under continuous laser diode (LD) irradiation for up to 60 min and zero quenching at temperatures as high as 205 °C. These properties are further validated in LD illumination applications. This work provides a novel strategy for the development of high-performance CDs-based phosphors with excellent photothermal stability and offers significant insights into the integration of CDs with porous materials. The findings hold considerable promise for advancing the design and utilization of CDs in solid-state lighting and display technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multicolor
In-situ carbonation
Confinement effect
Photothermal stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs washed from multicolor CDs@ZIF-8
Preparation of CDs@ZIF-8 with different reaction times
Morphology and structure of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8
Photothermal stability enhancement mechanism of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 with high stability prepared by an in-situ solvothermal method. SEM images of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8: (b) B-CDs@ZIF-8, (c) G-CDs@ZIF-8, (d) Y-CDs@ZIF-8, (e) O-CDs@ZIF-8, (f) R-CDs@ZIF-8, and (g) ZIF-8.

(a) XRD patterns of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8. (b) FTIR spectra of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8. (c) Wide-range XPS spectra of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8. High-resolution XPS spectra of (d) C1s, (e) N1s, and (f) Zn2p of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8.

(a) PL spectra of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8. (b) Optical images of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. (c) Fluorescence contour map of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 versus LD illumination time. (d) Fluorescence contour map of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 versus temperature.

TEM images of CDs obtained from multicolor CDs@ZIF-8, insets are HRTEM: (a) B-CDs, (b) G-CDs, (c) Y-CDs, (d) O-CDs, and (e) R-CDs. (f) TEM image of ZIF-8. (g) Schematic diagram of the luminescence mechanism of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8.

TEM images of CDs@ZIF-8 prepared at different reaction times: (a) 3, (b) 6, (c) 9, and (d) 12 h. TEM images of CDs obtained from CDs@ZIF-8 prepared at different reaction times, insets are HRTEM: (e) 3, (f) 6, (g) 9, and (h) 12 h. (i) Schematic diagram of the growth process of CDs@ZIF-8. (j) PL spectra of CDs@ZIF-8 prepared at different reaction times. (k) PL spectra of CDs obtained from CDs@ZIF-8 prepared at different reaction times.
(a) Schematic diagram of the fabrication of LD device. (b) Physical images of multicolor LD device. (c) Emission spectra of white LD device. d) CIE of white LD device.

head.ce:title: Revealing the in-situ growth mechanism of carbon dots confined in ZIF-8 as multicolor fluorescent material with high photothermal stability

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Forschungszentrum Jülich GmbH, Helmholtz-Institute Erlangen-Nürnberg (HI ERN), Department of High Throughput Methods in Photovoltaics, Immerwahrstraße 2, Erlangen 91058, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of High Throughput Methods in Photovoltaics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Forschungszentrum Jülich GmbH, Helmholtz-Institute Erlangen-Nürnberg (HI ERN), Department of High Throughput Methods in Photovoltaics, Immerwahrstraße 2, Erlangen 91058, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) based fluorescent materials offer tremendous potential for application in high-definition displays and advanced illumination systems. However, achieving solid-state CDs-based phosphors with robust photothermal stability remains a critical challenge. In this study, multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 phosphors were synthesized by precisely controlling the carbonation degree of CDs under the confinement effect of ZIF-8. Serving as both template and a protective substrate, ZIF-8 enables the in-situ carbonation of the carbon source into CDs embedded within its pores through a solvothermal process. Structural and compositional analyses following HCl etching of the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 phosphors showed that the in-situ growth of CDs in ZIF-8 pores. The multicolor emission of the CDs is attributed to the quantum size effect. The rigid structure of the ZIF-8 backbone imparts exceptional photothermal stability to the CDs@ZIF-8 phosphors. As a result, the materials exhibit stable fluorescence under continuous laser diode (LD) irradiation for up to 60 min and zero quenching at temperatures as high as 205 °C. These properties are further validated in LD illumination applications. This work provides a novel strategy for the development of high-performance CDs-based phosphors with excellent photothermal stability and offers significant insights into the integration of CDs with porous materials. The findings hold considerable promise for advancing the design and utilization of CDs in solid-state lighting and display technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as the excellent candidates in solid-state lighting owing to their excellent optoelectronic properties, such as tunable emission wavelength, simple synthesis, and cost effectiveness, have attracted extensive attention from numerous researchers [1–6]. However, resonance energy transfer and direct π–π interactions of CDs result in severe aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching (ACQ) issues [7–10]. In addition, different from conventional inorganic phosphors, CDs generally suffer from serious fluorescence thermal quenching, which thus greatly restricts their optoelectronic application [11–15]. In general, when a solid-state lighting device is in operation, the light source is highly energetic and the light beam is very concentrated, especially in the laser diode (LD) device, where laser light source irradiation to solid-state phosphor produces a large amount of heat, necessitating the phosphor to be photothermally stable to resist the degradation of the device [16,17]. Consequently, the challenges for CDs are their high fluorescence and structural stability in solid-state lighting.

Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) is a kind of MOFs with zeolite skeleton structure, and has tunable structure, high porosity, high thermal stability, high chemical stability, and facile preparation [18–21]. Among many MOFs materials, ZIF-8 can be chosen as the protective matrix for CDs, the three reasons are as follows. First, the high porosity of ZIF-8 plays an important role in uniform dispersion of CDs and effectively suppresses the ACQ phenomenon of CDs [22–24]. Second, the high chemical stability of ZIF-8 provides a stable chemical environment for CDs, which can significantly improve the fluorescence stability and long-term durability of CDs [25,26]. Third, the performance of CDs@ZIF-8 composites can be optimized by regulating the interaction between ZIF-8 and CDs [27–29]. Many efforts have successfully shown the great potential of ZIF-8 as a protective matrix in overcoming the stability and ACQ problem of CDs. For example, Li et al. obtained CDs@ZIF-8 phosphors with blue, green, and red emission by encapsulating three classes of CDs with ZIF-8 as a substrate for solid-state lighting [30]. Wang et al. produced a green CDs@ZIF-8 fluorescent probe by encapsulating CDs in ZIF-8, effectively preventing CDs aggregation. The as-prepared CDs@ZIF-8 maintains a stable fluorescence intensity after 60 min of UV irradiation, and shows only 9 % weight loss at 600 °C, demonstrating the excellent photothermal stability of CDs@ZIF-8 [31]. However, current research primarily focused on monochromatic luminescence, with little attention to the development of multicolor phosphors or a detailed investigation on the photothermal stability of CDs@ZIF-8 phosphors. In addition, the interaction mechanism between the ZIF-8 framework and embedded CDs still need be deeply investigated.

Here, an in-situ growth strategy of CDs in ZIF-8 is employed to achieve single system multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 phosphor with high photothermal stability. In-situ growth and luminescence mechanism of CDs in ZIF-8 pores is explored by regulating the reaction process. The formation of the rigid structure of ZIF-8 and the introduction of Zn2+ as an electronic defect in the CDs inhibit the thermal quenching of the CDs, resulting in the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 with excellent photothermal stability. Finally, the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 phosphors are applied to LD devices. This study provides insights for the development of multicolor CDs-based phosphors with high photothermal stability, and contributes to the advancement of CDs-based phosphors in the field of solid-state lighting.

o-Phenylenediamine (OPD), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), and 2-methylimidazole (2-MeIm) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Anhydrous methanol, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Deionized water was produced in our laboratory. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

First, certain amounts (0.9, 0.6, 0.5, 0.3, and 0.3 g) of OPD were weighed and dissolved in 7 mL of anhydrous methanol separately. Then five portions of 1.3 g of 2-MeIm were weighed and solved in 10 mL of anhydrous methanol by sonication. Subsequently, different amounts of OPD and 2-MeIm solutions were mixed and stirred for 10 min, and 5 mL of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (0.3 g) methanol solution was added separately, and the mixed solution was stirred for 8 h at 500 rpm. After 12 h of standing, the ZIF-8 composites encapsulating CDs precursors settled out at the bottom of the beaker, which were named p-OPD@ZIF-8.

First, 22 mL of DMF was added to the raw solution of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 precursors. The mixed solutions were then placed in a 100 mL Teflon autoclave for reaction at 220 °C for 6 h, 200 °C for 3 h, 240 °C for 6 h, 200 °C for 12 h, and 240 °C for 6 h, separately. The precipitates were obtained by centrifugation and washed with anhydrous methanol for three times and then dried in an oven at 50 °C overnight. The obtained CDs@ZIF-8 were named as B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-CDs@ZIF-8, respectively.

To confirm that the fluorescence of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 originates from the incorporation of CDs within ZIF-8, the sample were synthesized following the same reaction procedure as multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 but without extra carbon source. This sample is denoted as C@ZIF-8, where “C” refers to the carbon-containing products within the ZIF-8 pores through solvent or ligand carbonylation. Specifically, five portions of 1.3 g of 2-MeIm were weighed and dissolved in 10 mL of anhydrous methanol by sonication. Then, 5 mL of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (0.3 g) methanol solution was introduced and stirred at 500 rpm for 8 h. After being left to stand for 12 h, the mixed solution gave settled ZIF-8 at beaker bottom. Subsequently, 22 mL of DMF was added to the beaker, and the solutions were placed in a 100 mL Teflon autoclave and reacted at 220 °C for 6 h, at 200 °C for 3 h, at 240 °C for 6 h, at 200 °C for 12 h, and at 240 °C for 6 h, separately. Finally, the precipitates were obtained by centrifugation and washed with anhydrous methanol for three times, and then dried in an oven at 50 °C overnight. The resultant products were named B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-C@ZIF-8 respectively. Among them, Y- and R-C@ZIF-8 had the same reaction conditions and were the same product, so they were uniformly named Y,R-C@ZIF-8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs washed from multicolor CDs@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 was dispersed in 10 mL of anhydrous methanol to form a suspension, and HCl was added dropwise to the solution until the ZIF-8 framework was completely decomposed with no more precipitate in the solution. The solutions were dialyzed to remove decomposed ZIF-8 and HCl for 24 h. The CDs with blue, green, yellow, orange, and red emission were synthesized, and they are denoted as B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-CDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs@ZIF-8 with different reaction times
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process of this products was consistent with that of O-CDs@ZIF-8, with the difference that the solvothermal reaction time was controlled at 3, 6, 9, and 12 h, separately.

A certain weight of CDs@ZIF-8 was weighed and dispersed between the interlayers of two sapphire glass sheets, and the edges of the interlayers of the two sapphire glass sheets were filled with resin, so that the phosphor was encapsulated between the two sapphire glass sheets. An LD was fixed on a test bench, its positive and negative wires were connected to external power supply, and the sapphire glass sheets encapsulating phosphor were fixed on top of the LD to obtain an LD device.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were conducted on TESCAN Mira3 LMH and JEOL F200 microscopes, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired on a Panalytical Empyrean X-ray diffractometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Tensor 27. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was determined on an ESCALAB 250Xi instrument. Photoluminescence spectra (PL) and absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) were obtained on a FS5 spectrofluorometer. The time-resolved photoluminescence spectra (TRPL) were recorded with FLS 980 spectrometer. Thermogravimetric (TG) curves were measured on a Shimadzu DTG-60AH thermogravimetric analyzer. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra (UV–vis) were measured with Persee TU-1810 spectrophotometer. PL and quality parameters of LD device were measured by PR-655 Spectra Scan with the computer control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a shows the schematic illustration of the preparation of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8, where o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as a carbon source is encapsulated within the pores of ZIF-8, followed by in-situ solvothermal treatment to convert the carbon source in-situ into CDs within ZIF-8. With the confinement effect of ZIF-8 on CDs, the growth of CDs is tuned by varying the amount of carbon source, reaction temperature, and reaction time to obtain multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 with high photothermal stability. Firstly, the morphology of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). B-CDs@ZIF-8 are crystals around 1 μm in size (Figs. 1b and S1a). G-CDs@ZIF-8 (Figs. 1c and S1b) and R-CDs@ZIF-8 (Figs. 1f and S1e) consist of inhomogeneous particles with sizes of approximately 190 nm and 210 nm, respectively. Y-CDs@ZIF-8 (Figs. 1d and S1c) and O-CDs@ZIF-8 (Figs. 1e and S1d) present quasi-spherical particles with sizes of about 240 nm and 180 nm, respectively. It can be seen that the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 are affected by the carbon source and the solvothermal reaction, which make them more different from ZIF-8 with an average size of 140 nm. In addition, CDs and ZIF-8 are similarly contrast owing to the fact that the major element of both is C, resulting in the inability to differentiate the location of CDs in the TEM image of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8. Element mapping shows that the elements C, N, O, and Zn are uniformly distributed in the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. S2), and it is presumed that the CDs are uniformly dispersed in ZIF-8.

Further, the crystal structure and surface chemical composition of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 were characterized. Fig. 2 a shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8, where the characteristic diffraction peaks are consistent with those of simulated ZIF-8, and the diffraction peaks at 7.3°, 10.4°, 12.7°, 14.7°, 16.4°, 18.0°, 19.4°, 22.0°, 24.5°, and 26.7° correspond to the (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), (222), (123), (114), (233), and (134) crystal planes of ZIF-8, respectively [32–34]. This indicates that the crystal structure of ZIF-8 in multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 is not destroyed by the generation of CDs, while the diffraction peaks of CDs are not clearly observed owing to their weakness or overlap with the ZIF-8 diffraction peaks [29,35]. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 reveal the characteristic absorption bands of ZIF-8 (Fig. 2b). The absorption band at 421 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of Zn–N. The absorption bands located at 900–1350 cm−1 and 1350–1500 cm−1 are assigned to the bending and stretching vibrations of imidazole ring, respectively. The band at 1585 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of CN bond, and those at 2930 cm−1 and 3135 cm−1 to the CH stretching vibration of aliphatic and aromatic groups, respectively [36]. In addition, the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 show absorption bands at 1674 cm−1 and 1238 cm−1, which do not belong to ZIF-8, but originate from the stretching vibrations of the CO and COC bonds, respectively. Their presence may be attributed to the involvement of methanol in the carbonation of the carbon source within ZIF-8 pore to generate CDs with oxygen functional groups.

The full X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 were recorded to investigate the surface properties (Fig. 2c), where the signals of C1s (284.8 eV), N1s (398.9 eV), O1s (532.3 eV), and Zn2p (1022.2 eV) are clearly observed. It is worth noting that the Zn/N values of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 are basically the same (Table S1), indicating that the process of generating CDs from carbon sources is carried out in the interior of ZIF-8 rather than on the surface of ZIF-8, otherwise the Zn/N values would be varied according to the change of carbon source dosage, reaction temperature, and reaction time. Further, high-resolution XPS spectra of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 are shown in Figs. 2d–f and S3. In the C1s spectrum, the peaks at 284.7, 285.9, and 288.6 eV are attributed to CC/CC, CN/CO, and CO/CN, respectively [37–39]. The N1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks, CN (398.9 eV) and CN/NH (400 eV) [40]. The two peaks appearing in O1s are attributed to CO/ZnO (531.7 eV) and CO/OH (533.2 eV). The two peaks in the Zn2p spectrum are attributed to Zn2p3/2 (1022 eV) and Zn2p1/2 (1045 eV) [41]. The functional group types of the XPS result are consistent with those of the FTIR result.

Photoluminescence spectra (PL) of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 and optical pictures under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation are shown in Figs. 3 a, b and S4. The optimal emission wavelengths of B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-CDs@ZIF-8 are 462, 533, 553, 575, and 592 nm, corresponding to solid-state fluorescence of blue, green, yellow, orange, and red, respectively. The achievement of multicolor solid-state luminescence may be attributed to the different reaction parameters leading to different nucleation and growth rates of CDs within ZIF-8 pore channels, resulting in different morphologies, sizes, and surface states of CDs, and thus in-situ generation of CDs with different luminescence wavelengths. At the same time, the in-situ generated CDs are homogeneously dispersed with in ZIF-8 as a substrate in which they overcame ACQ to obtain solid-state luminescence. Subsequently, the time-resolved photoluminescence spectra (TRPL) of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 demonstrate average fluorescence lifetimes (τavg) of B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-CDs@ZIF-8 of 1.32, 2.37, 19.21, 21.55, and 23.56 ns, respectively (Fig. S5). The gradual increase in τavg with the redshift of emission wavelength indicates a decrease in non-radiative traps and an increase in fluorescent molecules, as is further confirmed by the increase in PLQY of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 (1.87 %, 3.16 %, 4.58 %, 5.49 %, 14.66 %) with the redshift of emission wavelength.

The photothermal stability of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 was investigated. First, the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 were continuously irradiated with LD, as shown in Figs. 3c and S6a. The fluorescence intensity of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 remains basically unchanged under LD irradiation for up to 60 min, the luminescence peak position is not significantly shifted, and the full width at half maximum is not changed. Then, the effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 was tested by exposing them to different temperatures, as shown in Figs. 3d and S6b. The fluorescence intensity of the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 shows a rise and then a decrease as the temperature increases. At the temperature of 205 °C, the fluorescence intensity of B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-CDs@ZIF-8 decreases by 28.9 %, 2.5 %, 15.9 %, 13.6 %, and 0, respectively. Finally, the thermogravimetric (TG) curves of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 were tested in N2 atmosphere. Fig. S7a exhibits TG curve of B-CDs@ZIF-8, where a weight loss of 17 % occurs in the temperature range of 150–300 °C, which originates from the volatilization of water, the removal of residual solutions and the breakage of certain chemical bonds of CDs. A 3 % weight loss between 350 and 400 °C, is caused by the decomposition of CDs. After 550 °C, the weight of B-CDs@ZIF-8 undergoes rapid decay owing to the decomposition of ZIF-8 ligand and the disruption of backbone structure. G-, Y-, O-, and R-CDs@ZIF-8 exhibit only 1.6 %, 5.2 %, 2.7 %, and 4.5 % weight loss due to volatilization of water and removal of small molecule impurities in the range of 100–520 °C, respectively (Fig. S7b–e). Conclusively, the photothermal stability of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 is superior to that of the majority of CDs phosphors (Table S2), which is mainly derived from the protective effect of ZIF-8 matrix on CDs.
In order to reveal the growth mechanism of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8, the morphology structure, luminescence mechanism, and growth process of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 are analyzed as follows:

First, the growth mechanism of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 is revealed from the perspective of exploring the reasons for the diverse morphology. Fig. S8 shows the SEM images of p-OPD@ZIF-8, which serve as a precursor for multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 (ZIF-8 encapsulating different contents of OPD). All p-OPD@ZIF-8 show uniform particles with a size of 140 nm, consistent with the size and morphology of ZIF-8, indicating that OPD does not affect the crystallization process of ZIF-8 at room temperature. Further, the morphology of C@ZIF-8 prepared from ZIF-8 without the addition of carbon source OPD under the same reaction conditions as those for multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 were investigated, as shown in Fig. S9. The morphology of B-C@ZIF-8 and OC@ZIF-8 is similar to that of O-CDs@ZIF-8 with quasi-spherical particles. G-C@ZIF-8 are irregular particles and Y,R-C@ZIF-8 are large size crystals. The larger size of C@ZIF-8 compared with ZIF-8 is attributed to the deprotonation of 2-MeIm on the surface of ZIF-8 under heating conditions enabling the crystals to continue to grow [42]. Since the reaction temperature of Y,R-C@ZIF-8 is the highest among the C@ZIF-8 (240 °C), the crystal size of Y,R-C@ZIF-8 is the largest. The amount of OPD in multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 decreases sequentially in the synthesis process, and when a small amount of OPD is contained in solution, −NH2 on the surface of OPD accelerates the deprotonation of ZIF-8 ligand [43] to form smaller sized CDs@ZIF-8, as shown in Fig. 1c–f. When the OPD content is high, the alkaline nature of OPD increases the pH of the solution, which leads to the complete deprotonation of 2-MeIm on the surface of ZIF-8, resulting in the growth of crystals in all directions [44] and the formation of larger crystals (Figs. 1b and S10). In addition, the FTIR band positions of p-OPD@ZIF-8 and C@ZIF-8 are basically consistent with those of ZIF-8 (Fig. S11), and the CO and COC bands of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 do not appear (Fig. 2b), further confirming that OPD in ZIF-8 is involved in the generation of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8.

Second, the luminescence mechanism of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 is explored. The fluorescence of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 is initially speculated to originate from the in-situ carbonization of OPD as a carbon source in ZIF-8 pore to generate different fluorescent CDs. In order to verify the speculation, the PL spectra of the solid-state product C@ZIF-8 prepared without OPD carbon source under the same reaction conditions as the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 were preferentially investigated. As shown in Fig. S12, the optimal emission wavelengths of B-, G-, O-, and Y, R-C@ZIF-8 are 442, 439, 435, and 428 nm, respectively. The blue emission may originate from the CDs generated by carbonylation of ZIF-8 ligands, thus confirming that the tunable emission wavelengths of the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 are related to the introduction of carbon source OPD, rather than being associated with the changes in the three-dimensional structure of ZIF-8. In order to further verify the in-situ carbonation of carbon source OPDs in ZIF-8 to generate CDs, HCl was used to etch the ZIF-8 in multicolor CDs@ZIF-8, and the CDs were washed out and characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 4 a–e. B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-CDs washed out from the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 show average particle sizes of 4.87, 6.28, 6.41, 6.67, and 6.78 nm, respectively, and their lattice spacings can be measured from the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) to be 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) of graphitic carbon [45]. It is noteworthy that the CDs washed out from the ZIF-8 skeleton still maintain the cluster structure resembling the outline of the ZIF-8 shape (Fig. 4f) and are not dispersed due by the sonication treatment. This may be due to the self-assembly behavior of CDs containing amino groups that can replace 2-MeIm to coordinate with Zn2+ at the original position after the disruption of ZIF-8 skeleton [46]. Subsequently, the emission wavelengths of B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-CDs are measured by PL to be 424, 519, 571, 580, and 650 nm, respectively (Fig. S13), consistent with the luminescence colors of their corresponding solid-state CDs@ZIF-8. Meanwhile, PL testing of CDs in the supernatant of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs prepared under the same reaction conditions as multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 shows that most of the luminescence peak positions are concentrated in yellow (Figs. S14 and S15). It is indicated that the luminescence of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 originates from the in-situ generation of CDs inside ZIF-8 rather than the adsorption of multicolor CDs on the surface, and the particle size of in-situ generated CDs varies with the change of the reaction parameters, proving that ZIF-8 plays a confinement effect on the growth of internal CDs. In addition, the first excitonic absorption bands of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 are 375, 415, 426, 435, and 460 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. S16. By comparison, it is found that the first excitonic absorption band and the emission wavelengths of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 increase sequentially with the increase in the size of the CDs in multicolor CDs@ZIF-8. The good linear relationship in Fig. S17 suggests that the tunable emission of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 originates from the quantum size effect of in-situ grown CDs inside ZIF-8 (Fig. 4g) [47].

Finally, the growth mechanism of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 is further deeply dissected by exploring the growth process of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 (the growth process of CDs in ZIF-8). O-CDs@ZIF-8 with the most uniform internal CDs morphology was chosen as the research object, and the products obtained at 3 h intervals during the preparation of O-CDs@ZIF-8 were tested and characterized. Figs. S18 and 5a–d show SEM and TEM images of CDs@ZIF-8 obtained at reaction times of 3, 6, 9, and 12 h, respectively. With the increase of reaction time, the deprotonation of 2-MeIm on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8 causes the gradual increase of CDs@ZIF-8 particles from 140 nm to 180 nm, with the particles cross-linked together. Subsequently, CDs were obtained from CDs@ZIF-8 prepared at different reaction times by HCl etching of ZIF-8. As shown in Fig. 5 e–h, the CDs are all of cluster structures with average particle sizes of 2.78, 3.20, 3.89, and 6.67 nm, respectively, exhibiting a pattern of gradual increase in the number of CDs, particle size, and cluster coverage with the increase of reaction time (Fig. 5i). In addition, the optimal emission wavelengths of this series of CDs@ZIF-8 are 521, 542, 557, and 575 nm (Fig. 5j), and the corresponding optimal emission wavelengths of the CDs are 518, 529, 531, and 580 nm, respectively (Fig. 5k). The results show that the particle size of CDs gradually increases with the prolongation of reaction time, while the emission wavelength of CDs undergoes a significant redshift, once again verifying that the quantum size effect of CDs plays a dominant role in the luminescence properties of CDs@ZIF-8.

In brief, the formation mechanism of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 is summarized as follows: under the confinement effect of ZIF-8, the particle size of CDs in ZIF-8 pore gradually increases with the increase of reaction time. At the same time, the emission wavelength of CDs is red-shifted, mainly owing to the quantum size effect of CDs dominating the luminescence properties of CDs@ZIF-8. In addition, the temperature and OPD content affect the deprotonation process of ZIF-8 ligand. When the temperature or the OPD content is higher, the size of CDs@ZIF-8 is larger, which makes the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 show varying morphological changes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal stability enhancement mechanism of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: The photothermal stability enhancement mechanism of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 is inferred from the structural characterization results of multicolor CDs@ZIF-8: On one hand, ZIF-8 ligand and carbon source OPD react with each other to generate CDs, and then covalent bonds are formed between the in-situ generated CDs and ZIF-8 to inhibit the rotation of ZIF-8 ligand, and thus to transform ZIF-8 into a rigid structure [48], which weakens the electron–phonon coupling effect to inhibit the non-radiative transitions [49], and ultimately inhibits the thermal quenching of the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. S19a). On the other hand, Zn2+ of ZIF-8 is doped into CDs during the growth of CDs and acts as an electron trap for CDs [50,51], as shown in Fig. S19b. Some electrons are induced to be trapped and stored in this trap energy level at low temperatures, and the trapped electrons can jump out of the trap energy level after thermal stimulation, and then the electrons are transferred to the excited state energy level through the conduction band to realize luminescence, so that the process of electron capture by the trap energy level and electron de-capture from the trap tends to be balanced, leading to even zero quenching phenomenon [52].

LD is a new type of solid-state lighting devices. Different from LED, it does not suffer from the problem of “efficiency droop”. Since the light source of LD device is a laser with high energy and concentrated beam, the laser light source will generate a lot of heat when irradiating the phosphor for a long period of time. Therefore, it is necessary to use a phosphor with excellent stability to construct LD device. Multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 were applied to LD devices as phosphors, as shown in Fig. 6 a and b, enabling multicolor LD devices to be constructed by adopting blue LD excitation with yellow phosphors, which is the mainstream method of fabricating white LDs. From the emission spectrum in Fig. 6c and d, it can be seen that the peak at 450 nm is attributed to the blue LD, and the emission peak at around 582 nm originates from Y-CDs@ZIF-8, whose emission position is red-shifted a little from the fluorescence emission peaks as a result of the testing errors of different testing instruments. The white LD device exhibits a CIE of (0.34, 0.30), a CRI of 82, and a CCT of 4947 K. Consequently, the multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 with high photothermal stability can withstand the irradiation of a laser light source, providing a new strategy for the development of multicolor solid-state lighting.

In summary, multicolor CDs@ ZIF-8 phosphors with high photothermal stability were synthesized by a ZIF-8 in situ confined CDs growth strategy. The emission peak positions of CDs@ZIF-8 phosphors are tunable from 462 to 592 nm by precisely regulating the reaction parameters. Exploring the in-situ growth mechanism of carbon dots in ZIF-8 pores by acid etching method reveals that the multicolor luminescence of CDs@ZIF-8 phosphor is attributed to the quantum size effect of CDs. The rigid structure of ZIF-8 effectively inhibits the thermal quenching of CDs and thus improves the photothermal stability of CDs@ZIF-8. Multicolor CDs@ZIF-8 with high photothermal stability can maintain stable fluorescence performance under continuous illumination (60 min) and at 205 °C, and can be applied to laser illumination devices. This work could be important for the development of efficient multicolor LDs.

Zhi Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Lingpeng Yan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhengshu Song: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiyun Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yongzhen Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization. Xuguang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2025.02.117.

ce:section.ce:para:
